title: What is the realistic wall-clock cost per task for a 3-browser × N-route × V-v
question: What is the realistic wall-clock cost per task for a 3-browser × N-route × V-viewport matrix using the existing preview-server approach, and what cap value (routes × viewports × browsers) prevents unacceptable run times?
findings: The UI Reviewer's per-task cost has two fixed components and one variable component. Fixed: dev-server startup (npm run dev in a git worktree) takes 5–20 s; git worktree add + teardown adds ~3 s; Playwright browser process launch adds ~2–4 s per browser engine (chromium slightly faster than webkit/firefox). Variable: each (route × viewport) visual-diff cell costs ~1–2 s (navigate to localhost ~0.5–1.5 s + page.screenshot ~0.2 s + compareVisual/pixelmatch ~0.1 s); each (route × axe-viewport) axe cell costs ~1–3 s (navigate + axe.run injection and eval). The UI Reviewer prompt structures browsers as the outer loop, meaning each browser gets its own launch+teardown cycle while sharing the single preview server. Under this model the wall-clock formula is approximately: T ≈ 20 s (server) + B × (3 s launch + R × V × 1.5 s screenshot) + R × 2 s (axe, chromium only), where B=browsers, R=routes, V=viewports. Illustrative estimates using the current 3-viewport config and the proposed 3-browser matrix: 1 route × 3 vp × 3 browsers ≈ 45 s; 3 routes × 3 vp × 3 browsers ≈ 72 s; 5 routes × 3 vp × 3 browsers ≈ 87 s (45 total cells); 10 routes × 3 vp × 3 browsers ≈ 155 s (90 cells); 15 routes × 3 vp × 3 browsers ≈ 220 s (135 cells); 20 routes × 3 vp × 3 browsers ≈ 340 s (180 cells, ~5.7 min). The existing 'all' crawl cap of 20 pages, when multiplied by the 3-browser matrix, yields 180 cells and pushes wall clock to ~5–6 min on a typical CI runner, which is marginal. Observed feedback envelopes (CLV-003, CLV-008) show the reviewer running 1 route × 3 viewports × 1 browser (chromium) with no recorded elapsed time, but the step count and findings are consistent with the 30–45 s estimate above. The reference-impl config ui-review.json defines exactly 3 viewports (mobile, tablet, desktop) and the consumer override at .cloverleaf/config/ui-review.json is identical in shape. No CI workflow in .github/workflows/ runs the UI Reviewer directly; it is invoked as an agent skill, so there is no CI-observed timing data. Key uncertainties: slower machines or heavy Astro dev-server pages could push per-cell cost to 3–4 s; webkit and firefox headless launch are sometimes 2× slower than chromium on Linux; pixelmatch cost scales with viewport pixel count (375×667=250 k px vs 1280×800=1024 k px, a 4× spread) but is still sub-200 ms even at desktop size.
recommendation: Set a hard cap of 90 total combinations (routes × viewports × browsers) as the default, with a configurable override key 'maxCombinations' in ui-review.json (defaulting to 90). At 90 cells the worst-case wall clock is approximately 155 s (~2.5 min), leaving comfortable headroom below a 5-minute agent-turn budget and well within typical 10-minute CI job limits. When the computed product R × V × B exceeds maxCombinations, the UI Reviewer should: (1) sort affected routes by recency or diff size (most-changed first), (2) take the first floor(maxCombinations / (V × B)) routes and skip the rest, and (3) emit one 'warning'-severity finding per skipped route with rule 'ui-review-cap' and message 'Route {route} skipped: combination count {R×V×B} exceeds maxCombinations ({cap}); review manually or raise the cap.' This keeps the finding actionable without blocking verdict. A secondary soft warning at 60 combinations (≈100 s) can alert teams that they are approaching the cap without triggering skips. Rationale for 90: it fits a typical 5-route × 3-viewport × 3-browser task (the most common realistic scope) with a 2× safety factor, and allows up to 10 routes before skipping begins, which covers the vast majority of feature-branch changes. Teams with faster machines or fewer viewports can raise the cap; teams with slow CI can lower it.
